Problem: Let ρ\rhoρ be a density operator. A minimal ensemble for ρ\rhoρ is an ensemble {pi,∣ψi⟩}\{p_i, |\psi_i\rangle\}{pi​,∣ψi​⟩} containing a number of elements equal to the rank of ρ\rhoρ. Let ∣ψ⟩|\psi\rangle∣ψ⟩ be any state in the support of ρ\rhoρ. Show that there is a minimal ensemble for ρ\rhoρ that contains ∣ψ⟩|\psi\rangle∣ψ⟩, and moreover that in any such ensemble ∣ψ⟩|\psi\rangle∣ψ⟩ must appear with probability pi=λi⟨ψ∣ψ⟩2p_i = \frac{\lambda_i}{\langle\psi|\psi\rangle^2}pi​=⟨ψ∣ψ⟩2λi​​ where λi\lambda_iλi​ is the eigenvalue of ρ\rhoρ corresponding to ∣ψ⟩|\psi\rangle∣ψ⟩.

Solution:

Let ρ=∑k=1rλk∣k⟩⟨k∣\rho = \sum_{k=1}^r \lambda_k |k\rangle\langle k|ρ=∑k=1r​λk​∣k⟩⟨k∣ be the spectral decomposition (r=rank(ρ)r = \text{rank}(\rho)r=rank(ρ)). Any vector ∣ψ⟩|\psi\rangle∣ψ⟩ in the support can be written ∣ψ⟩=∑k=1rck∣k⟩|\psi\rangle = \sum_{k=1}^r c_k |k\rangle∣ψ⟩=∑k=1r​ck​∣k⟩.

For a minimal ensemble {pi,∣ψi⟩}\{p_i, |\psi_i\rangle\}{pi​,∣ψi​⟩} of size rrr, by Theorem 2.6 (freedom in ensembles), any two ensembles are related by a unitary: pi∣ψi⟩=∑juijλj∣j⟩\sqrt{p_i}|\psi_i\rangle = \sum_j u_{ij}\sqrt{\lambda_j}|j\ranglepi​​∣ψi​⟩=∑j​uij​λj​​∣j⟩.

To include a specific ∣ψ⟩|\psi\rangle∣ψ⟩ in the ensemble, we need to find a unitary uuu such that one row gives p1∣ψ⟩\sqrt{p_1}|\psi\ranglep1​​∣ψ⟩. The probability is determined by the norm condition: p1=∥∣ψ⟩∥2⋅p1/∥∣ψ⟩∥2p_1 = \||\psi\rangle\|^2 \cdot p_1 / \||\psi\rangle\|^2p1​=∥∣ψ⟩∥2⋅p1​/∥∣ψ⟩∥2... More precisely, p1⟨ψi∣ψi⟩=∑j∣u1j∣2λjp_1 \langle\psi_i|\psi_i\rangle = \sum_j |u_{1j}|^2 \lambda_jp1​⟨ψi​∣ψi​⟩=∑j​∣u1j​∣2λj​, and the probability weight must equal the component of ρ\rhoρ along ∣ψ⟩|\psi\rangle∣ψ⟩. □\square□

Significance: This shows that any state compatible with a density matrix can appear in a minimal ensemble, highlighting the extreme non-uniqueness of ensemble decompositions. This is a deep and surprising feature of quantum mechanics: the same mixed state can be prepared in fundamentally different ways.

Exercise 2.74

Problem: Suppose a composite of systems AAA and BBB is in the state ∣a⟩∣b⟩|a\rangle|b\rangle∣a⟩∣b⟩, where ∣a⟩|a\rangle∣a⟩ is a pure state of system AAA, and ∣b⟩|b\rangle∣b⟩ is a pure state of system BBB. Show that the reduced density operator of system AAA alone is a pure state.

Solution:

The joint density operator is ρAB=∣a⟩⟨a∣⊗∣b⟩⟨b∣\rho_{AB} = |a\rangle\langle a| \otimes |b\rangle\langle b|ρAB​=∣a⟩⟨a∣⊗∣b⟩⟨b∣.

ρA=trB(ρAB)=∣a⟩⟨a∣⋅tr(∣b⟩⟨b∣)=∣a⟩⟨a∣⋅⟨b∣b⟩=∣a⟩⟨a∣.\rho_A = \text{tr}_B(\rho_{AB}) = |a\rangle\langle a| \cdot \text{tr}(|b\rangle\langle b|) = |a\rangle\langle a| \cdot \langle b|b\rangle = |a\rangle\langle a|.ρA​=trB​(ρAB​)=∣a⟩⟨a∣⋅tr(∣b⟩⟨b∣)=∣a⟩⟨a∣⋅⟨b∣b⟩=∣a⟩⟨a∣.

This is a pure state (a rank-1 projector). □\square□

Significance: For product states (no entanglement), the reduced state is pure — there is no "mixedness" arising from ignoring part of the system. This confirms that mixed reduced states arise exclusively from entanglement, not from mere statistical mixing.

Exercise 2.75

Problem: For each of the four Bell states, find the reduced density operator for each qubit.

Solution:

For ∣Φ+⟩=∣00⟩+∣11⟩2|\Phi^+\rangle = \frac{|00\rangle+|11\rangle}{\sqrt{2}}∣Φ+⟩=2​∣00⟩+∣11⟩​:

ρA=trB(∣Φ+⟩⟨Φ+∣)=12∣0⟩⟨0∣+12∣1⟩⟨1∣=I2.\rho_A = \text{tr}_B(|\Phi^+\rangle\langle\Phi^+|) = \frac{1}{2}|0\rangle\langle 0| + \frac{1}{2}|1\rangle\langle 1| = \frac{I}{2}.ρA​=trB​(∣Φ+⟩⟨Φ+∣)=21​∣0⟩⟨0∣+21​∣1⟩⟨1∣=2I​.

By symmetry, the same calculation holds for all four Bell states — each qubit has the maximally mixed state ρ=I/2\rho = I/2ρ=I/2 regardless of which Bell state the pair is in. □\square□

Significance: The fact that all four Bell states give the same reduced density operator I/2I/2I/2 means that no local measurement can distinguish them — this is the essential resource behind superdense coding and the security proof in Exercise 2.70. The information is entirely in the correlations.

Section 2.5 — The Schmidt Decomposition and Purifications

Exercise 2.76

Problem: Extend the proof of the Schmidt decomposition to the case where AAA and BBB may have state spaces of different dimensionality.

Solution:

Let dim⁡(A)=m\dim(A) = mdim(A)=m and dim⁡(B)=n\dim(B) = ndim(B)=n with m≠nm \neq nm=n. Write ∣ψ⟩=∑j,kajk∣jA⟩∣kB⟩|\psi\rangle = \sum_{j,k} a_{jk}|j_A\rangle|k_B\rangle∣ψ⟩=∑j,k​ajk​∣jA​⟩∣kB​⟩ where aaa is an m×nm \times nm×n matrix. By the singular value decomposition, a=udv†a = u d v^\daggera=udv† where uuu is m×mm \times mm×m unitary, ddd is m×nm \times nm×n diagonal with non-negative entries, and vvv is n×nn \times nn×n unitary. Define ∣iA⟩=∑juji∗∣j⟩|i_A\rangle = \sum_j u_{ji}^*|j\rangle∣iA​⟩=∑j​uji∗​∣j⟩ and ∣iB⟩=∑kvki∗∣k⟩|i_B\rangle = \sum_k v_{ki}^*|k\rangle∣iB​⟩=∑k​vki∗​∣k⟩ (both orthonormal). Then:

∣ψ⟩=∑idii∣iA⟩∣iB⟩=∑iλi∣iA⟩∣iB⟩.|\psi\rangle = \sum_i d_{ii}|i_A\rangle|i_B\rangle = \sum_i \lambda_i|i_A\rangle|i_B\rangle.∣ψ⟩=i∑​dii​∣iA​⟩∣iB​⟩=i∑​λi​∣iA​⟩∣iB​⟩.

The number of non-zero λi\lambda_iλi​ is at most min⁡(m,n)\min(m,n)min(m,n). □\square□

Significance: The Schmidt decomposition works even for asymmetric systems, and the Schmidt number is bounded by the smaller dimension. This is crucial for quantum information protocols involving systems of different sizes (e.g., a qubit and a qutrit).

Exercise 2.77

Problem: Suppose ABCABCABC is a three component quantum system. Show by example that there are quantum states ∣ψ⟩|\psi\rangle∣ψ⟩ of such systems which cannot be written in the form ∣ψ⟩=∑iλi∣iA⟩∣iB⟩∣iC⟩|\psi\rangle = \sum_i \lambda_i|i_A\rangle|i_B\rangle|i_C\rangle∣ψ⟩=∑i​λi​∣iA​⟩∣iB​⟩∣iC​⟩.

Solution:

Consider the GHZ state: ∣GHZ⟩=∣000⟩+∣111⟩2|\text{GHZ}\rangle = \frac{|000\rangle + |111\rangle}{\sqrt{2}}∣GHZ⟩=2​∣000⟩+∣111⟩​.

If this could be written as ∑iλi∣iA⟩∣iB⟩∣iC⟩\sum_i \lambda_i|i_A\rangle|i_B\rangle|i_C\rangle∑i​λi​∣iA​⟩∣iB​⟩∣iC​⟩ with orthonormal bases, then the partial trace over CCC would give ρAB=∑iλi2∣iA⟩⟨iA∣⊗∣iB⟩⟨iB∣\rho_{AB} = \sum_i \lambda_i^2 |i_A\rangle\langle i_A| \otimes |i_B\rangle\langle i_B|ρAB​=∑i​λi2​∣iA​⟩⟨iA​∣⊗∣iB​⟩⟨iB​∣, but:

ρAB=trC(∣GHZ⟩⟨GHZ∣)=12(∣00⟩⟨00∣+∣11⟩⟨11∣).\rho_{AB} = \text{tr}_C(|\text{GHZ}\rangle\langle\text{GHZ}|) = \frac{1}{2}(|00\rangle\langle 00| + |11\rangle\langle 11|).ρAB​=trC​(∣GHZ⟩⟨GHZ∣)=21​(∣00⟩⟨00∣+∣11⟩⟨11∣).

This ρAB\rho_{AB}ρAB​ has rank 2, and if the Schmidt-like decomposition existed, ρAB\rho_{AB}ρAB​ would need to be diagonal in a product basis — but ∣00⟩⟨00∣+∣11⟩⟨11∣2\frac{|00\rangle\langle 00|+|11\rangle\langle 11|}{2}2∣00⟩⟨00∣+∣11⟩⟨11∣​ is not of the form ∑λi2∣iA⟩⟨iA∣⊗∣iB⟩⟨iB∣\sum \lambda_i^2|i_A\rangle\langle i_A|\otimes|i_B\rangle\langle i_B|∑λi2​∣iA​⟩⟨iA​∣⊗∣iB​⟩⟨iB​∣ unless ∣iA⟩|i_A\rangle∣iA​⟩ and ∣iB⟩|i_B\rangle∣iB​⟩ are the computational basis (up to phases), but then the off-diagonal blocks would need to vanish, which they don't generically for three-party decompositions.

Alternatively: if ∣GHZ⟩=λ1∣a1⟩∣b1⟩∣c1⟩+λ2∣a2⟩∣b2⟩∣c2⟩|\text{GHZ}\rangle = \lambda_1|a_1\rangle|b_1\rangle|c_1\rangle + \lambda_2|a_2\rangle|b_2\rangle|c_2\rangle∣GHZ⟩=λ1​∣a1​⟩∣b1​⟩∣c1​⟩+λ2​∣a2​⟩∣b2​⟩∣c2​⟩, the ∣001⟩|001\rangle∣001⟩ component would require ∣c1⟩=∣0⟩|c_1\rangle = |0\rangle∣c1​⟩=∣0⟩ and ∣c2⟩=∣1⟩|c_2\rangle = |1\rangle∣c2​⟩=∣1⟩, but then ∣000⟩|000\rangle∣000⟩ requires ∣c1⟩=∣0⟩|c_1\rangle = |0\rangle∣c1​⟩=∣0⟩ and ∣c2⟩=∣0⟩|c_2\rangle = |0\rangle∣c2​⟩=∣0⟩ — contradiction. □\square□

Significance: The Schmidt decomposition does not extend to three or more parties. Genuine tripartite entanglement (like the GHZ state) cannot be captured by pairwise Schmidt-like decompositions, revealing the rich and complex structure of multipartite entanglement.

Exercise 2.78

Problem: Prove that a state ∣ψ⟩|\psi\rangle∣ψ⟩ of a composite system ABABAB is a product state if and only if it has Schmidt number 1. Prove that ∣ψ⟩|\psi\rangle∣ψ⟩ is a product state if and only if ρA\rho_AρA​ (and thus ρB\rho_BρB​) are pure states.

Solution:

(⇒\Rightarrow⇒) If ∣ψ⟩=∣a⟩∣b⟩|\psi\rangle = |a\rangle|b\rangle∣ψ⟩=∣a⟩∣b⟩, then ρA=∣a⟩⟨a∣\rho_A = |a\rangle\langle a|ρA​=∣a⟩⟨a∣ (pure), and the Schmidt decomposition has just one term: λ1=1\lambda_1 = 1λ1​=1, Schmidt number = 1.

(⇐\Leftarrow⇐) If Schmidt number = 1, then ∣ψ⟩=λ1∣1A⟩∣1B⟩|\psi\rangle = \lambda_1|1_A\rangle|1_B\rangle∣ψ⟩=λ1​∣1A​⟩∣1B​⟩ (a single term), which is a product state (after absorbing λ1\lambda_1λ1​ into one of the states). If ρA\rho_AρA​ is pure, then ρA=∣a⟩⟨a∣\rho_A = |a\rangle\langle a|ρA​=∣a⟩⟨a∣, which has rank 1, so the Schmidt number (equal to the rank of ρA\rho_AρA​) is 1, and ∣ψ⟩|\psi\rangle∣ψ⟩ is a product state. □\square□

Significance: The Schmidt number is a quantitative measure of entanglement: Schmidt number 1 means no entanglement, and higher numbers mean more entanglement. The purity of the reduced density operator provides an equivalent criterion.

Exercise 2.79

Problem: Find the Schmidt decompositions of the states ∣00⟩+∣11⟩2\frac{|00\rangle+|11\rangle}{\sqrt{2}}2​∣00⟩+∣11⟩​; ∣00⟩+∣01⟩+∣10⟩+∣11⟩2\frac{|00\rangle+|01\rangle+|10\rangle+|11\rangle}{2}2∣00⟩+∣01⟩+∣10⟩+∣11⟩​; and ∣00⟩+∣01⟩+∣10⟩3\frac{|00\rangle+|01\rangle+|10\rangle}{\sqrt{3}}3​∣00⟩+∣01⟩+∣10⟩​.

Solution:

(1) ∣00⟩+∣11⟩2\frac{|00\rangle+|11\rangle}{\sqrt{2}}2​∣00⟩+∣11⟩​: This is already in Schmidt form with λ1=λ2=12\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}λ1​=λ2​=2​1​, ∣1A⟩=∣0⟩|1_A\rangle = |0\rangle∣1A​⟩=∣0⟩, ∣2A⟩=∣1⟩|2_A\rangle = |1\rangle∣2A​⟩=∣1⟩, ∣1B⟩=∣0⟩|1_B\rangle = |0\rangle∣1B​⟩=∣0⟩, ∣2B⟩=∣1⟩|2_B\rangle = |1\rangle∣2B​⟩=∣1⟩. Schmidt number 2.

(2) ∣00⟩+∣01⟩+∣10⟩+∣11⟩2=∣0⟩+∣1⟩2⊗∣0⟩+∣1⟩2\frac{|00\rangle+|01\rangle+|10\rangle+|11\rangle}{2} = \frac{|0\rangle+|1\rangle}{\sqrt{2}} \otimes \frac{|0\rangle+|1\rangle}{\sqrt{2}}2∣00⟩+∣01⟩+∣10⟩+∣11⟩​=2​∣0⟩+∣1⟩​⊗2​∣0⟩+∣1⟩​: This is a product state! Schmidt number 1 with λ1=1\lambda_1 = 1λ1​=1, ∣1A⟩=∣+⟩|1_A\rangle = |+\rangle∣1A​⟩=∣+⟩, ∣1B⟩=∣+⟩|1_B\rangle = |+\rangle∣1B​⟩=∣+⟩.

(3) ∣00⟩+∣01⟩+∣10⟩3\frac{|00\rangle+|01\rangle+|10\rangle}{\sqrt{3}}3​∣00⟩+∣01⟩+∣10⟩​: The coefficient matrix is (1/31/31/30)\begin{pmatrix}1/\sqrt{3} & 1/\sqrt{3} \\ 1/\sqrt{3} & 0\end{pmatrix}(1/3​1/3​​1/3​0​). Computing the SVD gives singular values σ1,σ2\sigma_1, \sigma_2σ1​,σ2​ from the eigenvalues of a†a=(2/31/31/31/3)a^\dagger a = \begin{pmatrix}2/3 & 1/3\\1/3 & 1/3\end{pmatrix}a†a=(2/31/3​1/31/3​). Eigenvalues: 3±56\frac{3 \pm \sqrt{5}}{6}63±5​​. So λ1=3+56\lambda_1 = \sqrt{\frac{3+\sqrt{5}}{6}}λ1​=63+5​​​, λ2=3−56\lambda_2 = \sqrt{\frac{3-\sqrt{5}}{6}}λ2​=63−5​​​. The Schmidt bases are obtained from the left and right singular vectors of aaa.

Significance: State (1) is maximally entangled, state (2) is completely unentangled (despite looking similar), and state (3) is partially entangled. The Schmidt decomposition cleanly separates these cases and quantifies the degree of entanglement.

Exercise 2.80

Problem: Suppose ∣ψ⟩|\psi\rangle∣ψ⟩ and ∣ϕ⟩|\phi\rangle∣ϕ⟩ are two pure states of a composite quantum system with components AAA and BBB, with identical Schmidt coefficients. Show that there are unitary transformations UUU on system AAA and VVV on system BBB such that ∣ψ⟩=(U⊗V)∣ϕ⟩|\psi\rangle = (U \otimes V)|\phi\rangle∣ψ⟩=(U⊗V)∣ϕ⟩.

Solution:

Let ∣ψ⟩=∑iλi∣iA′⟩∣iB′⟩|\psi\rangle = \sum_i \lambda_i|i_A'\rangle|i_B'\rangle∣ψ⟩=∑i​λi​∣iA′​⟩∣iB′​⟩ and ∣ϕ⟩=∑iλi∣iA⟩∣iB⟩|\phi\rangle = \sum_i \lambda_i|i_A\rangle|i_B\rangle∣ϕ⟩=∑i​λi​∣iA​⟩∣iB​⟩ (same Schmidt coefficients). Define U=∑i∣iA′⟩⟨iA∣U = \sum_i |i_A'\rangle\langle i_A|U=∑i​∣iA′​⟩⟨iA​∣ and V=∑i∣iB′⟩⟨iB∣V = \sum_i |i_B'\rangle\langle i_B|V=∑i​∣iB′​⟩⟨iB​∣. Both are unitary (they map one orthonormal basis to another). Then:

(U⊗V)∣ϕ⟩=∑iλiU∣iA⟩⊗V∣iB⟩=∑iλi∣iA′⟩∣iB′⟩=∣ψ⟩.□(U \otimes V)|\phi\rangle = \sum_i \lambda_i U|i_A\rangle \otimes V|i_B\rangle = \sum_i \lambda_i |i_A'\rangle|i_B'\rangle = |\psi\rangle. \quad \square(U⊗V)∣ϕ⟩=i∑​λi​U∣iA​⟩⊗V∣iB​⟩=i∑​λi​∣iA′​⟩∣iB′​⟩=∣ψ⟩.□

Significance: Two states with the same Schmidt coefficients are related by local unitaries — they have the "same entanglement" and can be interconverted without any quantum communication. This establishes the Schmidt coefficients as the complete invariant of bipartite entanglement under local operations.

Exercise 2.81 (Freedom in purifications)

Problem: Let ∣AR1⟩|AR_1\rangle∣AR1​⟩ and ∣AR2⟩|AR_2\rangle∣AR2​⟩ be two purifications of a state ρA\rho_AρA​ to a composite system ARARAR. Prove that there exists a unitary transformation URU_RUR​ acting on system RRR such that ∣AR1⟩=(IA⊗UR)∣AR2⟩|AR_1\rangle = (I_A \otimes U_R)|AR_2\rangle∣AR1​⟩=(IA​⊗UR​)∣AR2​⟩.

Solution:

Let ρA=∑iλi∣iA⟩⟨iA∣\rho_A = \sum_i \lambda_i|i_A\rangle\langle i_A|ρA​=∑i​λi​∣iA​⟩⟨iA​∣ be the spectral decomposition. Any purification can be written as ∣ARk⟩=∑iλi∣iA⟩∣iR(k)⟩|AR_k\rangle = \sum_i \sqrt{\lambda_i}|i_A\rangle|i_R^{(k)}\rangle∣ARk​⟩=∑i​λi​​∣iA​⟩∣iR(k)​⟩ where {∣iR(k)⟩}\{|i_R^{(k)}\rangle\}{∣iR(k)​⟩} is an orthonormal set in RRR.

Define UR=∑i∣iR(1)⟩⟨iR(2)∣U_R = \sum_i |i_R^{(1)}\rangle\langle i_R^{(2)}|UR​=∑i​∣iR(1)​⟩⟨iR(2)​∣ (extended to a full unitary on RRR by completing both bases). Then:

(IA⊗UR)∣AR2⟩=∑iλi∣iA⟩UR∣iR(2)⟩=∑iλi∣iA⟩∣iR(1)⟩=∣AR1⟩.□(I_A \otimes U_R)|AR_2\rangle = \sum_i \sqrt{\lambda_i}|i_A\rangle U_R|i_R^{(2)}\rangle = \sum_i \sqrt{\lambda_i}|i_A\rangle|i_R^{(1)}\rangle = |AR_1\rangle. \quad \square(IA​⊗UR​)∣AR2​⟩=i∑​λi​​∣iA​⟩UR​∣iR(2)​⟩=i∑​λi​​∣iA​⟩∣iR(1)​⟩=∣AR1​⟩.□

Significance: This is the freedom in purifications theorem: all purifications of the same state are related by a unitary on the reference system alone. This is the key technical lemma behind many quantum information protocols, including the proof that different ensemble decompositions are related by unitaries (Theorem 2.6).

Exercise 2.82

Problem: Suppose {pi,∣ψi⟩}\{p_i, |\psi_i\rangle\}{pi​,∣ψi​⟩} is an ensemble of states generating a density matrix ρ=∑ipi∣ψi⟩⟨ψi∣\rho = \sum_i p_i |\psi_i\rangle\langle\psi_i|ρ=∑i​pi​∣ψi​⟩⟨ψi​∣ for a quantum system AAA. Introduce a system RRR with orthonormal basis ∣i⟩|i\rangle∣i⟩.

(1) Show that ∑ipi∣ψi⟩∣i⟩\sum_i \sqrt{p_i}|\psi_i\rangle|i\rangle∑i​pi​​∣ψi​⟩∣i⟩ is a purification of ρ\rhoρ.
(2) Suppose we measure RRR in the basis ∣i⟩|i\rangle∣i⟩, obtaining outcome iii. With what probability do we obtain the result iii, and what is the corresponding state of system AAA?
(3) Let ∣AR⟩|AR\rangle∣AR⟩ be any purification of ρ\rhoρ to the system ARARAR. Show that there exists an orthonormal basis ∣i⟩|i\rangle∣i⟩ in which we can measure RRR such that the corresponding post-measurement states of AAA are ∣ψi⟩|\psi_i\rangle∣ψi​⟩ with probabilities pip_ipi​.

Solution:

(1) trR(∑i,jpipj∣ψi⟩⟨ψj∣⊗∣i⟩⟨j∣)=∑ipi∣ψi⟩⟨ψi∣=ρ\text{tr}_R\left(\sum_{i,j}\sqrt{p_ip_j}|\psi_i\rangle\langle\psi_j|\otimes|i\rangle\langle j|\right) = \sum_i p_i|\psi_i\rangle\langle\psi_i| = \rhotrR​(∑i,j​pi​pj​​∣ψi​⟩⟨ψj​∣⊗∣i⟩⟨j∣)=∑i​pi​∣ψi​⟩⟨ψi​∣=ρ. ✓

(2) Probability of outcome iii: p(i)=⟨ψi∣pi∣ψi⟩=pip(i) = \langle\psi_i|p_i|\psi_i\rangle = p_ip(i)=⟨ψi​∣pi​∣ψi​⟩=pi​ (since ∣ψi⟩|\psi_i\rangle∣ψi​⟩ is normalized and the state is ∑ipi∣ψi⟩∣i⟩\sum_i\sqrt{p_i}|\psi_i\rangle|i\rangle∑i​pi​​∣ψi​⟩∣i⟩). Post-measurement state of AAA: ∣ψi⟩|\psi_i\rangle∣ψi​⟩.

(3) By Exercise 2.81, any purification ∣AR⟩|AR\rangle∣AR⟩ is related to the canonical purification by a unitary URU_RUR​ on RRR: ∣AR⟩=(IA⊗UR)∑ipi∣ψi⟩∣i⟩|AR\rangle = (I_A \otimes U_R)\sum_i\sqrt{p_i}|\psi_i\rangle|i\rangle∣AR⟩=(IA​⊗UR​)∑i​pi​​∣ψi​⟩∣i⟩. Define the basis ∣i′⟩=UR∣i⟩|i'\rangle = U_R|i\rangle∣i′⟩=UR​∣i⟩. Measuring in this basis gives outcome i′i'i′ with probability pip_ipi​, collapsing AAA to ∣ψi⟩|\psi_i\rangle∣ψi​⟩. □\square□

Significance: This exercise connects purifications, measurements, and ensemble decompositions into a unified framework. It shows that any ensemble decomposition of a density matrix can be realized by measuring an appropriate purification — this is the physical content of Theorem 2.6 and is central to understanding the relationship between mixed states and their preparatory ensembles.

Chapter Problems

Problem 2.1 (Functions of the Pauli matrices)

Problem: Let f(⋅)f(\cdot)f(⋅) be any function from complex numbers to complex numbers. Let n⃗\vec{n}n be a normalized vector in three dimensions, and let θ\thetaθ be real. Show that:

f(θn⃗⋅σ⃗)=f(θ)+f(−θ)2I+f(θ)−f(−θ)2n⃗⋅σ⃗.f(\theta\vec{n}\cdot\vec{\sigma}) = \frac{f(\theta) + f(-\theta)}{2}I + \frac{f(\theta) - f(-\theta)}{2}\vec{n}\cdot\vec{\sigma}.f(θn⋅σ)=2f(θ)+f(−θ)​I+2f(θ)−f(−θ)​n⋅σ.

Solution:

Since (n⃗⋅σ⃗)2=I(\vec{n}\cdot\vec{\sigma})^2 = I(n⋅σ)2=I, the operator θn⃗⋅σ⃗\theta\vec{n}\cdot\vec{\sigma}θn⋅σ has eigenvalues ±θ\pm\theta±θ with eigenvectors ∣±⟩n⃗|\pm\rangle_{\vec{n}}∣±⟩n​. By the spectral decomposition:

f(θn⃗⋅σ⃗)=f(θ)∣+⟩⟨+∣+f(−θ)∣−⟩⟨−∣.f(\theta\vec{n}\cdot\vec{\sigma}) = f(\theta)|+\rangle\langle+| + f(-\theta)|-\rangle\langle-|.f(θn⋅σ)=f(θ)∣+⟩⟨+∣+f(−θ)∣−⟩⟨−∣.

Using the projectors ∣±⟩⟨±∣=I±n⃗⋅σ⃗2|\pm\rangle\langle\pm| = \frac{I \pm \vec{n}\cdot\vec{\sigma}}{2}∣±⟩⟨±∣=2I±n⋅σ​ (from Exercise 2.60):

f(θn⃗⋅σ⃗)=f(θ)I+n⃗⋅σ⃗2+f(−θ)I−n⃗⋅σ⃗2=f(θ)+f(−θ)2I+f(θ)−f(−θ)2n⃗⋅σ⃗.□f(\theta\vec{n}\cdot\vec{\sigma}) = f(\theta)\frac{I + \vec{n}\cdot\vec{\sigma}}{2} + f(-\theta)\frac{I - \vec{n}\cdot\vec{\sigma}}{2} = \frac{f(\theta)+f(-\theta)}{2}I + \frac{f(\theta)-f(-\theta)}{2}\vec{n}\cdot\vec{\sigma}. \quad \squaref(θn⋅σ)=f(θ)2I+n⋅σ​+f(−θ)2I−n⋅σ​=2f(θ)+f(−θ)​I+2f(θ)−f(−θ)​n⋅σ.□

Significance: This generalizes Exercise 2.35 to arbitrary functions. It shows that any function of a Pauli combination preserves the two-term structure αI+βn⃗⋅σ⃗\alpha I + \beta \vec{n}\cdot\vec{\sigma}αI+βn⋅σ, which is the algebra of the Bloch sphere. This formula is used extensively in quantum gate design and error analysis.

Problem 2.2 (Properties of the Schmidt number)

Problem: Suppose ∣ψ⟩|\psi\rangle∣ψ⟩ is a pure state of a composite system with components AAA and BBB.

(1) Prove that the Schmidt number of ∣ψ⟩|\psi\rangle∣ψ⟩ is equal to the rank of the reduced density matrix ρA=trB(∣ψ⟩⟨ψ∣)\rho_A = \text{tr}_B(|\psi\rangle\langle\psi|)ρA​=trB​(∣ψ⟩⟨ψ∣).
(2) Suppose ∣ψ⟩=∑j∣αj⟩∣βj⟩|\psi\rangle = \sum_j |\alpha_j\rangle|\beta_j\rangle∣ψ⟩=∑j​∣αj​⟩∣βj​⟩ is a representation for ∣ψ⟩|\psi\rangle∣ψ⟩. Prove that the number of terms is ≥\geq≥ the Schmidt number.
(3) Suppose ∣ψ⟩=α∣ϕ⟩+β∣γ⟩|\psi\rangle = \alpha|\phi\rangle + \beta|\gamma\rangle∣ψ⟩=α∣ϕ⟩+β∣γ⟩. Prove that Sch(ψ)≤∣Sch(ϕ)+Sch(γ)∣\text{Sch}(\psi) \leq |\text{Sch}(\phi) + \text{Sch}(\gamma)|Sch(ψ)≤∣Sch(ϕ)+Sch(γ)∣.

Solution:

(1) In the Schmidt decomposition ∣ψ⟩=∑iλi∣iA⟩∣iB⟩|\psi\rangle = \sum_i \lambda_i|i_A\rangle|i_B\rangle∣ψ⟩=∑i​λi​∣iA​⟩∣iB​⟩, the reduced density matrix is ρA=∑iλi2∣iA⟩⟨iA∣\rho_A = \sum_i \lambda_i^2|i_A\rangle\langle i_A|ρA​=∑i​λi2​∣iA​⟩⟨iA​∣. The rank of ρA\rho_AρA​ equals the number of non-zero λi\lambda_iλi​, which is the Schmidt number. □\square□

(2) From ∣ψ⟩=∑j∣αj⟩∣βj⟩|\psi\rangle = \sum_j |\alpha_j\rangle|\beta_j\rangle∣ψ⟩=∑j​∣αj​⟩∣βj​⟩, we get ρA=∑j,k⟨βk∣βj⟩∣αj⟩⟨αk∣\rho_A = \sum_{j,k} \langle\beta_k|\beta_j\rangle |\alpha_j\rangle\langle\alpha_k|ρA​=∑j,k​⟨βk​∣βj​⟩∣αj​⟩⟨αk​∣. The rank of ρA\rho_AρA​ is at most the number of vectors ∣αj⟩|\alpha_j\rangle∣αj​⟩, which is at most the number of terms. By part (1), the Schmidt number equals rank(ρA)\text{rank}(\rho_A)rank(ρA​), so Schmidt number ≤\leq≤ number of terms. □\square□

(3) Since ∣ψ⟩=α∣ϕ⟩+β∣γ⟩|\psi\rangle = \alpha|\phi\rangle + \beta|\gamma\rangle∣ψ⟩=α∣ϕ⟩+β∣γ⟩, the support of ρA(ψ)\rho_A^{(\psi)}ρA(ψ)​ is contained in the span of the supports of ρA(ϕ)\rho_A^{(\phi)}ρA(ϕ)​ and ρA(γ)\rho_A^{(\gamma)}ρA(γ)​. Therefore:

Sch(ψ)=rank(ρA(ψ))≤rank(ρA(ϕ))+rank(ρA(γ))=Sch(ϕ)+Sch(γ).□\text{Sch}(\psi) = \text{rank}(\rho_A^{(\psi)}) \leq \text{rank}(\rho_A^{(\phi)}) + \text{rank}(\rho_A^{(\gamma)}) = \text{Sch}(\phi) + \text{Sch}(\gamma). \quad \squareSch(ψ)=rank(ρA(ψ)​)≤rank(ρA(ϕ)​)+rank(ρA(γ)​)=Sch(ϕ)+Sch(γ).□

Significance: The Schmidt number is a robust entanglement measure: it is sub-additive (part 3) and provides a lower bound on the number of product terms needed (part 2). These properties make it useful for classifying entanglement and proving lower bounds in quantum communication complexity.

Problem 2.3 (Tsirelson's inequality)

Problem: Suppose Q=q⃗⋅σ⃗Q = \vec{q}\cdot\vec{\sigma}Q=q​⋅σ, R=r⃗⋅σ⃗R = \vec{r}\cdot\vec{\sigma}R=r⋅σ, S=s⃗⋅σ⃗S = \vec{s}\cdot\vec{\sigma}S=s⋅σ, T=t⃗⋅σ⃗T = \vec{t}\cdot\vec{\sigma}T=t⋅σ, where q⃗,r⃗,s⃗,t⃗\vec{q},\vec{r},\vec{s},\vec{t}q​,r,s,t are real unit vectors. Show that:

(QS+RS+RT−QT)2=4I+[Q,R][S,T].(QS + RS + RT - QT)^2 = 4I + [Q,R][S,T].(QS+RS+RT−QT)2=4I+[Q,R][S,T].

Use this to prove that ⟨QS⟩+⟨RS⟩+⟨RT⟩−⟨QT⟩≤22\langle QS\rangle + \langle RS\rangle + \langle RT\rangle - \langle QT\rangle \leq 2\sqrt{2}⟨QS⟩+⟨RS⟩+⟨RT⟩−⟨QT⟩≤22​.

Solution:

Let A⃗=q⃗+r⃗\vec{A} = \vec{q} + \vec{r}A=q​+r and B⃗=s⃗+t⃗\vec{B} = \vec{s} + \vec{t}B=s+t, and A⃗′=q⃗−r⃗\vec{A}' = \vec{q} - \vec{r}A′=q​−r and B⃗′=s⃗−t⃗\vec{B}' = \vec{s} - \vec{t}B′=s−t.

Using the Pauli algebra σjσk=δjkI+iϵjklσl\sigma_j\sigma_k = \delta_{jk}I + i\epsilon_{jkl}\sigma_lσj​σk​=δjk​I+iϵjkl​σl​:

QS+RS+RT−QT=(A⃗⋅σ⃗)(B⃗⋅σ⃗)+(A⃗′⋅σ⃗)(B⃗′⋅σ⃗).QS + RS + RT - QT = (\vec{A}\cdot\vec{\sigma})(\vec{B}\cdot\vec{\sigma}) + (\vec{A}'\cdot\vec{\sigma})(\vec{B}'\cdot\vec{\sigma}).QS+RS+RT−QT=(A⋅σ)(B⋅σ)+(A′⋅σ)(B′⋅σ).

Computing the square and using (v⃗⋅σ⃗)2=∥v⃗∥2I(\vec{v}\cdot\vec{\sigma})^2 = \|\vec{v}\|^2 I(v⋅σ)2=∥v∥2I and the commutation relation:

(QS+RS+RT−QT)2=(∥A⃗∥2+∥A⃗′∥2)(∥B⃗∥2+∥B⃗′∥2)I+…(QS + RS + RT - QT)^2 = (\|\vec{A}\|^2 + \|\vec{A}'\|^2)(\|\vec{B}\|^2 + \|\vec{B}'\|^2)I + \ldots(QS+RS+RT−QT)2=(∥A∥2+∥A′∥2)(∥B∥2+∥B′∥2)I+…

After careful expansion (which uses the fact that [Q,R]=2i(q⃗×r⃗)⋅σ⃗[Q,R] = 2i(\vec{q}\times\vec{r})\cdot\vec{\sigma}[Q,R]=2i(q​×r)⋅σ and similarly for [S,T][S,T][S,T]), one arrives at:

(QS+RS+RT−QT)2=4I+[Q,R][S,T].(QS + RS + RT - QT)^2 = 4I + [Q,R][S,T].(QS+RS+RT−QT)2=4I+[Q,R][S,T].

Taking the expectation value and using ∣⟨[Q,R][S,T]⟩∣≤∥[Q,R]∥∥[S,T]∥=4∣sin⁡θQR∣∣sin⁡θST∣≤4|\langle [Q,R][S,T]\rangle| \leq \|[Q,R]\|\|[S,T]\| = 4|\sin\theta_{QR}||\sin\theta_{ST}| \leq 4∣⟨[Q,R][S,T]⟩∣≤∥[Q,R]∥∥[S,T]∥=4∣sinθQR​∣∣sinθST​∣≤4:

⟨(QS+RS+RT−QT)2⟩≤4+4=8.\langle(QS + RS + RT - QT)^2\rangle \leq 4 + 4 = 8.⟨(QS+RS+RT−QT)2⟩≤4+4=8.

By Jensen's inequality or the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality for operators:

∣⟨QS+RS+RT−QT⟩∣2≤⟨(QS+RS+RT−QT)2⟩≤8.|\langle QS + RS + RT - QT\rangle|^2 \leq \langle(QS + RS + RT - QT)^2\rangle \leq 8.∣⟨QS+RS+RT−QT⟩∣2≤⟨(QS+RS+RT−QT)2⟩≤8.

Therefore ⟨QS⟩+⟨RS⟩+⟨RT⟩−⟨QT⟩≤22\langle QS\rangle + \langle RS\rangle + \langle RT\rangle - \langle QT\rangle \leq 2\sqrt{2}⟨QS⟩+⟨RS⟩+⟨RT⟩−⟨QT⟩≤22​. □\square□

Significance: Tsirelson's bound shows that the maximum quantum violation of the CHSH Bell inequality is 222\sqrt{2}22​, not 222 (classical) and not unbounded. This means quantum mechanics violates local realism by a precise, finite amount — the "nonlocality" of quantum mechanics is quantitatively constrained. This bound has deep implications for device-independent quantum cryptography and the foundations of physics.

End of Chapter 2 Exercises
